stem: A 24-year-old woman in her first pregnancy presents at 9 weeks gestation with persistent vomiting since 6 weeks, unable to keep fluids down for 36 hours. She has lost 4 kg from her booking weight and reports dizziness on standing. Urine ketones are 3+. She has tried dietary modification, small frequent meals, and ginger without benefit. Pulse rate is 110 and blood pressure 96/58 standing.

data_table.Vital signs: HR 110 supine 124 standing, BP 96/58, T 36.9, RR 18, SpO2 99%
data_table.Examination: Dry mucous membranes, mild epigastric tenderness, no thyroid mass, no abdominal mass
data_table.Investigations: Urine ketones 3+, sodium 132 mmol/L, potassium 3.2 mmol/L, beta hCG 78000 IU/L; TFTs pending; ultrasound singleton pregnancy

lead_in: What is the most appropriate initial management?

options.text: Single dose intramuscular ondansetron 8 mg and discharge home with a 7 day script for ondansetron
options.rationale: Discharge home does not address dehydration and electrolyte derangement requiring intravenous fluids and inpatient assessment. (Source: RANZCOG: Nausea and vomiting in pregnancy)
source_refs: RANZCOG: Nausea and vomiting in pregnancy

options.text: Outpatient oral metoclopramide 10 mg three times daily with general practitioner review tomorrow
options.rationale: Ketonuria, electrolyte derangement and inability to tolerate oral fluids exceed outpatient management. (Source: RANZCOG: Nausea and vomiting in pregnancy)
source_refs: RANZCOG: Nausea and vomiting in pregnancy

options.text: Admit for intravenous Hartmann solution with thiamine 100 mg IV and oral pyridoxine doxylamine
options.rationale: Hyperemesis with ketonuria, electrolyte derangement and dehydration requires IV rehydration with thiamine cover plus first-line antiemetic pyridoxine doxylamine. (Source: RANZCOG: Nausea and vomiting in pregnancy)
source_refs: RANZCOG: Nausea and vomiting in pregnancy

options.text: Oral prochlorperazine 5 mg three times daily and ginger 250 mg four times daily for 3 days
options.rationale: Outpatient oral antiemetic therapy is inadequate when oral intake cannot be maintained and dehydration is significant. (Source: RANZCOG: Nausea and vomiting in pregnancy)
source_refs: RANZCOG: Nausea and vomiting in pregnancy

options.text: Termination of pregnancy counselling because of failure of conservative management at 9 weeks
options.rationale: Termination is not the first-line response to inadequate ambulatory care of nausea and vomiting; admission and escalation of antiemetic therapy precede this discussion. (Source: RANZCOG: Nausea and vomiting in pregnancy)
source_refs: RANZCOG: Nausea and vomiting in pregnancy

explanation.summary: Hyperemesis gravidarum is severe nausea and vomiting in pregnancy with weight loss greater than 5%, dehydration, ketonuria, and electrolyte disturbance. Initial admission management is intravenous Hartmann or normal saline rehydration with potassium replacement and thiamine 100 mg intravenously to prevent Wernicke encephalopathy before any glucose-containing fluids. First-line antiemetic in AU practice is oral or rectal pyridoxine doxylamine, with metoclopramide, prochlorperazine, ondansetron, and corticosteroids in escalation. Recurrent presentations prompt screening for molar pregnancy, multiple pregnancy, and thyroid dysfunction.
key_points: IV rehydration with thiamine and first-line pyridoxine doxylamine address dehydration and ketonuria
Outpatient management fails when oral intake cannot be maintained or significant ketonuria is present
Recurrent admissions trigger ultrasound and thyroid function review to exclude molar or multiple pregnancy
explanation.pearls: Thiamine 100 mg IV is given before any glucose containing fluid in hyperemesis to prevent Wernicke encephalopathy, which is reported and avoidable in modern practice.

sources.label: RANZCOG: Nausea and vomiting in pregnancy
SOMANZ: Nausea and vomiting in pregnancy

subtopic_detail: Asymptomatic bacteriuria detected at antenatal booking
stem: A 28-year-old woman attends her antenatal booking visit at 13 weeks gestation. She is asymptomatic. The midstream urine culture from booking grows greater than 10 to the 8 colony forming units per litre of Escherichia coli sensitive to nitrofurantoin, cefalexin and trimethoprim. She has no allergies and no known renal disease. Pregnancy has otherwise been uncomplicated.

data_table.Examination: Soft non-tender abdomen, no flank tenderness, no suprapubic tenderness
data_table.Investigations: MSU E. coli >10^8 CFU/L; urine dipstick leucocytes 1+ nitrites positive; renal function normal

lead_in: What is the most appropriate management?

options.text: Oral trimethoprim 300 mg daily for 3 days regardless of trimester or folate status
options.rationale: Trimethoprim is avoided in the first trimester because of folate antagonism and theoretical teratogenic risk. (Source: eTG Antibiotic: asymptomatic bacteriuria in pregnancy)
source_refs: eTG Antibiotic: asymptomatic bacteriuria in pregnancy

options.text: Repeat the MSU in 6 weeks before deciding to treat asymptomatic bacterial growth
options.rationale: Delaying treatment increases the risk of progression to pyelonephritis and adverse pregnancy outcomes. (Source: eTG Antibiotic: asymptomatic bacteriuria in pregnancy)
source_refs: eTG Antibiotic: asymptomatic bacteriuria in pregnancy

options.text: Single dose oral fosfomycin 3 g sachet without further test of cure because of pregnancy
options.rationale: Single dose fosfomycin is less effective in pregnancy than a longer course and test of cure is routinely recommended. (Source: eTG Antibiotic: asymptomatic bacteriuria in pregnancy)
source_refs: eTG Antibiotic: asymptomatic bacteriuria in pregnancy

options.text: Oral nitrofurantoin 100 mg four times daily for 5 days with repeat MSU after treatment
options.rationale: Asymptomatic bacteriuria in pregnancy is treated with a 5 to 7 day antibiotic course because of pyelonephritis and preterm birth risk, with test of cure. (Source: eTG Antibiotic: asymptomatic bacteriuria in pregnancy)
source_refs: eTG Antibiotic: asymptomatic bacteriuria in pregnancy

options.text: Continuous low dose nitrofurantoin 50 mg nightly until 36 weeks gestation without test of cure
options.rationale: Continuous prophylaxis is reserved for recurrent UTI in pregnancy after treatment, not a single positive booking culture. (Source: eTG Antibiotic: asymptomatic bacteriuria in pregnancy)
source_refs: eTG Antibiotic: asymptomatic bacteriuria in pregnancy

explanation.summary: Asymptomatic bacteriuria affects 2 to 10% of pregnant women and increases the risk of pyelonephritis, preterm birth, and low birth weight. AU practice screens all women at their antenatal booking with MSU culture and treats positive cultures with a 5 to 7 day antibiotic course (nitrofurantoin first or second trimester, cefalexin throughout) followed by test of cure 1 to 2 weeks after completion. Nitrofurantoin is avoided in the third trimester because of neonatal haemolysis risk; trimethoprim is avoided in the first trimester. Recurrent infection prompts consideration of low dose prophylaxis for the remainder of pregnancy.
key_points: Antenatal MSU screening detects asymptomatic bacteriuria and treatment reduces pyelonephritis and preterm birth
Nitrofurantoin and cefalexin are common AU first-line options with trimester restrictions
Test of cure 1 to 2 weeks after treatment is routine
explanation.pearls: Nitrofurantoin is avoided after 36 weeks gestation because of neonatal G6PD-related haemolysis risk; cefalexin or amoxicillin clavulanate is preferred at term.

sources.label: eTG Antibiotic: asymptomatic bacteriuria in pregnancy

subtopic_detail: Endometrial polyp on TVUS in a postmenopausal woman
stem: A 62-year-old postmenopausal woman presents with a single episode of postmenopausal bleeding 6 weeks ago. She has not bled since. Transvaginal ultrasound shows a thickened endometrium of 9 mm with a discrete focal lesion suggestive of an endometrial polyp. She has no significant medical history, is not on tamoxifen, and has a BMI of 26. She has a normal cervical screening history.

data_table.Examination: Pelvic examination normal, no cervical lesion on speculum, no adnexal mass
data_table.Investigations: TVUS endometrium 9 mm with discrete focal hyperechoic lesion 12 mm consistent with polyp; CA-125 normal

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Refer for total laparoscopic hysterectomy without prior histological diagnosis being obtained
options.rationale: Hysterectomy without histology over treats benign polyps which account for most postmenopausal bleeding lesions. (Source: RANZCOG: Postmenopausal bleeding)

options.text: Repeat transvaginal ultrasound in 6 months without immediate biopsy or hysteroscopy
options.rationale: Repeat imaging without histology in a woman with postmenopausal bleeding and a focal lesion delays diagnosis of malignancy. (Source: RANZCOG: Postmenopausal bleeding)

options.text: Pipelle endometrial biopsy alone as outpatient procedure without removing the polyp
options.rationale: Blind Pipelle biopsy misses focal lesions and is inadequate when a polyp is identified on ultrasound. (Source: RANZCOG: Postmenopausal bleeding)

options.text: Initiate continuous combined hormone replacement therapy to thin the endometrium
options.rationale: Hormone therapy does not address postmenopausal bleeding with a focal lesion and may worsen by masking pathology. (Source: RANZCOG: Postmenopausal bleeding)

options.text: Outpatient hysteroscopy and polyp resection with directed endometrial biopsy
options.rationale: Postmenopausal bleeding with a focal endometrial lesion is investigated with hysteroscopic resection and directed endometrial sampling to exclude malignancy. (Source: RANZCOG: Postmenopausal bleeding)

explanation.summary: Postmenopausal bleeding warrants exclusion of endometrial cancer. Transvaginal ultrasound measures endometrial thickness; a threshold of 4 mm or less reliably excludes cancer in women on no hormone therapy. Above this threshold, or in the presence of a focal lesion such as an endometrial polyp, hysteroscopy with directed biopsy and polypectomy is the AU standard of care. Most polyps are benign, but 5 to 10% in postmenopausal women harbour atypia or carcinoma. Asymptomatic polyps under 1 cm in premenopausal women may be observed.
key_points: Postmenopausal bleeding with endometrium over 4 mm or a focal lesion requires histology
Hysteroscopy with directed biopsy and polypectomy beats blind Pipelle for focal lesions
Polyps in postmenopausal women carry a 5 to 10% risk of malignancy
explanation.pearls: Endometrial thickness under 4 mm on TVUS in a postmenopausal woman with bleeding has a negative predictive value of around 99% for endometrial cancer when there is no focal lesion.

subtopic_detail: Threatened miscarriage in early first trimester
stem: A 31-year-old woman with a desired pregnancy presents at 7 weeks 3 days by last menstrual period with a 2 day history of light per-vaginal spotting and mild cramping. She has no fever, no shoulder tip pain, and her observations are normal. Transvaginal ultrasound shows an intrauterine gestation sac with a yolk sac and a 5 mm crown-rump length embryo with cardiac activity present at 110 beats per minute. The cervix is closed on speculum examination.

data_table.Examination: Soft abdomen, no peritonism, no shoulder tip pain, closed cervix on speculum
data_table.Investigations: Beta hCG 18000 IU/L, blood group O Rh positive; TVUS intrauterine gestation, CRL 5 mm, FHR 110 bpm, no subchorionic haematoma

lead_in: What is the most appropriate management?

options.text: Reassurance, pelvic rest, follow up ultrasound in 1 to 2 weeks if symptoms continue
options.rationale: Threatened miscarriage with confirmed viable intrauterine pregnancy is managed with reassurance and planned follow up imaging; most viable pregnancies continue. (Source: RANZCOG: Early pregnancy bleeding)

options.text: Immediate dilatation and curettage to clear products and prevent further bleeding
options.rationale: Dilatation and curettage is for retained products of conception or completed miscarriage, not a viable intrauterine pregnancy. (Source: RANZCOG: Early pregnancy bleeding)

options.text: Vaginal progesterone 400 mg twice daily for all women with threatened miscarriage and bleeding
options.rationale: Vaginal progesterone is only supported by AU practice for women with previous miscarriages presenting with bleeding, not all women. (Source: RANZCOG: Early pregnancy bleeding)

options.text: Anti-D 250 IU intramuscular as a single dose given to this Rh-positive woman today
options.rationale: Anti-D is only indicated in Rh-negative women at risk of sensitising events. (Source: RANZCOG: Anti-D)

options.text: Methotrexate 50 mg per m2 intramuscularly to manage a suspected ectopic pregnancy
options.rationale: Methotrexate is reserved for confirmed ectopic pregnancy without contraindications, not an intrauterine pregnancy. (Source: RANZCOG: Ectopic pregnancy)

explanation.summary: Threatened miscarriage is bleeding in early pregnancy with a closed cervix and confirmed viable intrauterine pregnancy. Most viable pregnancies (around 85 to 90%) continue to a live birth. Management is supportive: reassurance, pelvic rest, paracetamol for cramping, and review with repeat ultrasound at 1 to 2 weeks. Anti-D is given to Rh-negative women with significant bleeding. Vaginal progesterone has a modest benefit only in women with a history of one or more previous miscarriages presenting with current bleeding.
key_points: Confirmed viable intrauterine pregnancy with threatened miscarriage usually continues; manage supportively
Anti-D is given to Rh negative women with bleeding
Vaginal progesterone is selective for women with previous miscarriage history per AU practice
explanation.pearls: The PRISM trial demonstrated vaginal progesterone reduces miscarriage in women with current bleeding AND at least one prior miscarriage, with no benefit in women with no prior loss.

subtopic_detail: Postpartum contraception in a breastfeeding mother
stem: A 32-year-old woman is reviewed 4 weeks after an uncomplicated spontaneous vaginal delivery of her first child. She is exclusively breastfeeding and her child is gaining weight well. She and her partner want effective contraception and are willing to consider any method including long-acting reversible contraception. She has no history of venous thromboembolism, hypertension, or migraine with aura. Her smoking status is non-smoker.

data_table.Examination: Postnatal examination unremarkable, fundus involuted, no abnormal bleeding

lead_in: What is the most appropriate first-line contraceptive option?

options.text: Levonorgestrel intrauterine system inserted at 6 weeks postpartum is delayed by current uterine state
options.rationale: Although LNG-IUS is appropriate after 4 weeks postpartum, it is not contraindicated and is a reasonable alternative if patient prefers an IUS over an implant. (Source: RANZCOG: Contraception postpartum)

options.text: Combined oral contraceptive pill 30 micrograms ethinylestradiol started today as effective option
options.rationale: Combined hormonal contraception is avoided until at least 6 weeks postpartum and longer in breastfeeding because of VTE and possible milk supply effects. (Source: RANZCOG: Contraception postpartum)

options.text: Lactational amenorrhoea method alone as exclusive contraception without other supplementary method
options.rationale: Lactational amenorrhoea provides about 98% protection only with exclusive breastfeeding and amenorrhoea in the first 6 months, and is unreliable beyond. (Source: RANZCOG: Contraception postpartum)

options.text: Etonogestrel subdermal implant inserted today with timely follow up arranged for monitoring
options.rationale: Progestogen-only methods are safe in breastfeeding from any time postpartum and are first-line LARC for breastfeeding mothers without contraindications. (Source: RANZCOG: Contraception postpartum)

options.text: Diaphragm fitted in clinic today as primary method without backup contraceptive arrangements
options.rationale: Diaphragm efficacy is lower than LARC and fitting may need adjustment after pregnancy; not first-line. (Source: RANZCOG: Contraception postpartum)

explanation.summary: Postpartum contraception in breastfeeding women favours progestogen-only methods because they do not affect milk supply. The etonogestrel subdermal implant can be inserted from any time postpartum (including the early postnatal hospital stay), and intrauterine systems are usually placed at 4 weeks or more after delivery. Combined hormonal contraception is delayed until at least 6 weeks postpartum and considered cautiously in breastfeeding because of possible reduction in milk supply and VTE risk. The lactational amenorrhoea method offers about 98% effectiveness only when all three criteria are met: full breastfeeding, amenorrhoea, and infant under 6 months.
key_points: Progestogen-only methods are safe in breastfeeding from any time postpartum
Combined hormonal contraception is delayed until at least 6 weeks postpartum and considered cautiously in breastfeeding
LAM requires exclusive breastfeeding, amenorrhoea, and infant under 6 months for efficacy
explanation.pearls: Etonogestrel implant can be inserted any time postpartum including before hospital discharge and provides 3 years of effective contraception without affecting milk supply.
